segments.paradiso-8-g41:
E quanta e quale vid' io lei far piùe per allegrezza nova che s'accrebbe,
- E: coordinating conjunction 'and'.
- quanta e quale: a paired exclamation meaning 'how great and what kind' (quanta = 'how much/what degree'; quale = 'what kind/type'), used together for emphasis before the verb. 
- vid' io: vid' is the archaic/poetic truncation of vidi (passato remoto, 'I saw'); the subject io is repeated for emphasis. 
- lei far piùe: lei is the object 'her/she'; far (infinitive) is used after vidi to mean 'to make/become' — here 'to become' or 'to grow (more)'; piùe is an archaic/spellings variant of più 'more' (the -e is poetic). 
- per allegrezza nova che s'accrebbe: per = 'by/through'; allegrezza nova = 'a new joy'; che s'accrebbe is a relative clause: che = 'that' and s' (si) + accrebbe (accrescebbe/crebbe) = 'grew/increased within her' (reflexive si indicates the state growing in herself).
segments.paradiso-8-g42:
quando parlai,
- quando = 'when' introducing the temporal clause. 
- parlai is the first-person passato remoto of parlare ('I spoke'); here it gives the time when the speaker observed the change described above.
segments.paradiso-8-g43:
a l’allegrezze sue!
- a = 'to/for' marking direction or reference (here: directed at/connected with her rejoicings). 
- l' (il) + allegrezze = the plural noun 'the joys/rejoicings' (allegrezze is plural form used here). 
- sue = possessive 'her' (agreeing with allegrezze), so 'to/for her rejoicings.' The whole phrase emphasizes that the speaker's words produced increase specifically in her own delight.
segments.paradiso-8-g44: An absolute participial phrase: Così (adverb) + fatta (feminine past participle of fare) = "thus made" or "having been thus." The feminine participle agrees with the (implicit) feminine subject (Beatrice) and introduces the circumstance in which she speaks.
segments.paradiso-8-g45: mi (indirect object) + disse (passato remoto of dire) = "she said to me." Disse is the simple past (preterite) used here to report spoken words; the enclitic mi indicates the utterance is directed at the narrator.
segments.paradiso-8-g46: Il mondo = subject "the world." m' ebbe is mi ebbe (mi + ebbe, passato remoto of avere) meaning "the world had/held me." giù = "down/below" (indicating the earthly sphere); poco tempo = "a short time." The verb avere is used in the sense of possession or keeping here.
segments.paradiso-8-g47: A conditional clause introduced by e se + fosse stato (pluperfect subjunctive of essere): "and if I had been longer." The auxiliary+past participle (fosse stato) forms the counterfactual past ("had been"); subject (I) is omitted but understood from context.
segments.paradiso-8-g48: molto = "much/a great deal." sarà is future of essere ("there will be"); di mal = "of evil/harm" (mal = harm/evil). The construction states the expected consequenc e in the future/factual register: "there will be much harm."
segments.paradiso-8-g49: che introduces the subordinate clause: che non sarebbe = "that would not be." Non is simple negation; sarebbe is the conditional of essere ("would be"). Note the sequence futura/condizionale here produces a hypothetical shading: "there would be much harm that (otherwise) would not be."
segments.paradiso-8-g50: Full sentence: La mia letizia (subject: "my joy") + mi ti tien celato uses stacked clitic pronouns (mi = to me, ti = you) before the archaic present-tense form tien (modern tiene) + celato (past participle used adjectivally) = "my joy keeps you hidden from me." che introduces a relative/explanatory clause: mi raggia dintorno (raggiare "to radiate/suffuse," present) and mi nasconde (present "hides me"). quasi + animal di sua seta fasciato is a simile: "as if (quasi) an animal wrapped (fasciato) in its own silk (di sua seta)" — fasciato is a past participle describing the animal-image.
segments.paradiso-8-g51: Assai = adverb "much/ greatly." m'amasti is mi + amasti (passato remoto, 2nd person singular of amare) = "you loved me." The simple past (amasti) addresses past fact about the addressee's love.
segments.paradiso-8-g52: e = "and." avesti ben onde is an idiomatic clause: avesti (passato remoto of avere, "you had") + ben (intensifier "well/indeed") + onde (here = "cause/reason" — archaic use). Together: "and you had good reason." Note that the listed lemma senses for avesti/onde are not the ones used here (archaic/idiomatic meaning).
segments.paradiso-8-g53: che introduces the explanatory clause "for/because." s'io = se io (contraction) = "if I." fossi ... stato = imperfect subjunctive + past participle used as pluperfect subjunctive form (fosse/fossi stato) = "had been." giù = "down/below." The clause is counterfactual about the past: "for if I had been down there..."
segments.paradiso-8-g54: io ti mostrava: io (subject) + ti (direct object) + mostrava (imperfect indicative of mostrare) = "I would have shown you" (the imperfect here continues the counterfactual narrative). di mio amor = "of my love" (di + mio + amor). più oltre = comparative/adverbial phrase "further/more beyond" (più = more, oltre = beyond). che le fronde = "than the leaves" or "beyond the foliage" (here che introduces the comparison with 'le fronde' = the leaves/foliage); fronde is used as the image of leafy veil.
segments.paradiso-8-g94: Questo (demonstrative pronoun) introduces what the speaker just said; io is the subject pronoun 'I'; a + lui is the prepositional phrase 'to him' (a = to, lui = him). Word order 'Questo io a lui' is emphatic: 'This I (said) to him.'
segments.paradiso-8-g95: ed = e with the e- form before a vowel, 'and'; elli here functions as a subject meaning 'he' (an older/pronounced form of egli), followed by a me = 'to me' (to + me). The clause is elliptical: 'and he (said) to me'.
segments.paradiso-8-g96: S’io is the contraction of Se io ('If I'); this is an archaic elision where the e is dropped before a vowel. posso is present indicative 'I can' (first person singular). mostrarti is an infinitive (mostrare) with the enclitic object pronoun -ti 'to you' attached, so 'to show you'; un vero = 'a truth' or 'a true thing' (noun phrase governed by mostrarti).
segments.paradiso-8-g97: a quel che tu dimandi is a relative/complement clause: a = 'to' or 'with regard to', quel che = 'that which', tu dimandi = 'you ask' (present indicative). terrai is future indicative, second person singular 'you will hold/keep'; lo viso = 'the face' with the definite article lo; come tien lo dosso is a comparative clause 'as you hold/keep the back' where tien is present indicative 'you keep' and lo dosso (article + dosso) means 'the back' (archaic noun).
segments.paradiso-8-g98: Lo ben = 'the good' (lo + ben), where lo is the article and ben is a substantive 'good' or 'goodness.' che introduces a relative clause 'that.' tutto il regno che tu scandi links 'all the realm that you scandi/measure/traverse' (scandi is archaic present 'you scan/traverse'); volge e contenta are two present verbs ('turns' or 'directs' and 'satisfies/makes content') describing what that good does to the realm.
segments.paradiso-8-g99: fa esser = a causative periphrasis where fa (present 'makes') + infinitive esser 'to be' yields 'makes (it) be' or 'causes to be'; virtute sua provedenza = 'virtue (by) its providence' with sua modifying provedenza; in questi corpi grandi = prepositional phrase 'in these great bodies' indicating the realm in which providence's virtue is manifested.
segments.paradiso-8-g100: E non pur = 'And not only' (pur here meaning 'even/only' in a negative construction). le nature provedute = 'the natures provided/ordained' (provedute is a past participle functioning as adjective). sono in la mente = present indicative 'are in the mind' (in + the definite article la before mente), ch’è da sé perfetta = relative clause 'which is perfect of itself' (ch’ = che elided, è = is, da sé = by itself).
segments.paradiso-8-g101: ma = 'but'; esse (here plural) refers back to le nature 'they' (esse is the old/poetic third-person plural pronoun/form, not the infinitive), insieme con la lor salute = 'together with their salvation' (insieme = together, con = with, la lor = their, salute = salvation/health). The clause states that these ordered natures coexist with their own well-being/salvation.
segments.paradiso-8-g102: per che = 'therefore/for which reason'; quantunque = 'although'; quest’ arco saetta = 'this bow's arrow' (quest’ = quest + elision, arco = bow, saetta = projectile/arrow here); disposto = past participle 'disposed/arranged/prepared'; cade = present indicative 'falls'; a proveduto fine = 'to a provident/foreseen end' (proveduto = provided/ordained, fine = end/purpose). The clause expresses that even if the shot is an arrow from a bow, it nevertheless falls to a provided purpose.
segments.paradiso-8-g103: sì come = 'just as'; cosa in suo segno diretta = 'a thing directed to its sign/mark' (cosa = thing, in suo segno = to/into its mark/target, diretta = past participle 'directed'). The simile compares the arrow's falling to an object reaching the mark to which it was aimed.
segments.paradiso-8-g104: Se introduces a conditional; fosse is the imperfect subjunctive (3rd sing.) of essere used here for a counterfactual/hypothetical condition. The phrase means literally “If this were not so.”
segments.paradiso-8-g105: Il ciel is the subject (‘the heaven’ or celestial sphere). Che begins a relative clause modifying ciel; tu cammine is a present subjunctive/poetic present referring to ‘you’ moving through that heaven. Producerebbe is the 3rd-person singular conditional (would produce) and sì here is an adverbial intensifier forming a so…that result with the following che-clause; li suoi effetti is the direct object (‘its effects’).
segments.paradiso-8-g106: The che here introduces the result clause triggered by the preceding sì; non + sarebbero is the conditional negative (they would not be). Arti is a plural noun (‘arts’ or ordered skills) contrasted with the following noun.
segments.paradiso-8-g107: Ma is the adversative conjunction ‘but’; ruine is a plural noun meaning ‘ruins’ or ‘disasters.’ The short phrase completes the contrast begun in the previous clause.
segments.paradiso-8-g108: This is an inversion common in older/poetic Italian: standard order would be “ciò non può esser.” E connects to the previous clause; ciò is the demonstrative ‘that/this’; esser is the infinitive ‘to be’; non può is the present indicative (3rd sing.) of potere meaning ‘it cannot be’ or ‘that cannot happen.’
segments.paradiso-8-g109: Se begins another conditional (‘if’). Li ’ntelletti is the definite article plus plural noun ‘the intellects’ as subject; che introduces the relative clause modifying ’ntelletti. Muovon is an archaic/metrical form of muovono (3rd pl. present) ‘they move’ and queste stelle is the object of that verb. Non son manchi negates the predicate: son (sono) = ‘are’ and manchi = ‘lacking,’ so ‘are not lacking.’
segments.paradiso-8-g110: E is the coordinating conjunction ‘and.’ Manco here is an adverb meaning ‘less’ or ‘much less’ (i.e. ‘still less’ / ‘much less so’) modifying il primo (‘the first one’), referring back to the highest/intended antecedent; it intensifies the contrast just made.
segments.paradiso-8-g111: Che is a relative pronoun referring to il primo. Non + ha is the present indicative of avere (3rd sing.) negated; li is the clitic direct-object pronoun ‘them’ (referring to the preceding plural entity), and perfetti is an adjective placed after the verb meaning ‘perfect.’ The clause says the first one ‘does not have them perfect.’
segments.paradiso-8-g123: This segment contains only the punctuation mark (a full stop) used by Dante to close the previous utterance; there is no lexical material to analyse.
segments.paradiso-8-g124: Sì is the straightforward affirmative interjection 'yes' and is set off by the quotation marks and comma; it functions as the reply's opening particle.
segments.paradiso-8-g125: rispuos' is an archaic, apocopated form of the past-tense verb 'rispose/risposi' (from rispondere) and here is a 1st-person narrative past ('I answered'). The subject pronoun io follows as an emphatic subject; the apostrophe marks the omitted vowel.
segments.paradiso-8-g126: This reported-speech clause: 'e' = 'and' (coordination); 'qui' is an adverb meaning 'here' or 'in this matter/at this point'; 'ragion' is the noun 'reason/argument' as direct object; 'non' negates; 'cheggio' is an archaic present first-person form (from chiedere) meaning 'I ask' — so literally 'and here I ask no argument' (i.e. 'I don't demand an argument here').
segments.paradiso-8-g127: 'E' = 'and' joining this question to what precedes; 'puot'' is an archaic contracted form of potere (3rd-person singular), meaning 'can/ may/ could'; 'elli' here is an archaic subject pronoun (egli/egli = 'he/it' or impersonal 'it'), and 'esser' is the infinitive 'to be' — together forming the formula 'can it be / could it be'. Word order (verb + subject + infinitive) reflects older/poetic syntax.
segments.paradiso-8-g128: 'se' introduces a conditional clause ('if'); 'giù' is an adverb meaning 'down' or 'down below' (i.e. on earth); 'non' negates the verb; 'si vive' is an impersonal/passive construction with si + 3rd-person singular of vivere meaning 'one/people live' or 'people live'; 'diversamente' is the adverb 'differently'; 'per' here means 'by/through/because of'; 'diversi' modifies 'offici' (plural) and 'offici' means 'offices/duties/roles'. The whole clause asks whether the supposed fact could hold if, down on earth, people do not live differently according to (or because of) different offices.
segments.paradiso-8-g129: Non here is a blunt negative, functioning as an interjection ('No,') that begins the reply.
segments.paradiso-8-g130: 'se' = 'if' introducing the conditional; '’l' is the elided definite article 'il' before maestro; 'maestro vostro' is the noun phrase 'your master/teacher' with the possessive after the noun (a normal older/poetic order); 'ben' is an adverb 'well' or 'truly' modifying scrive; 'vi' is the dative/indirect-object pronoun 'to you' (or 'for you'/'in your tradition'); 'scrive' is 3rd-person singular present 'writes' or 'records'. Together: 'No, if your master writes it well.'
segments.paradiso-8-g112: Vuo’ is the archaic/poetic form of vuoi (second-person singular of volere) with the apostrophe marking the missing vowel; it means “do you want”. che introduces a subordinate clause and requires the subjunctive here. questo ver = “this truth/this true thing,” with ver (poetic for verità/‘truth’) as the noun being referred to. più precedes the clitic ti and means “any more/further.” ti is the second-person clitic meaning “to you/for you.” s’ is the contracted si of the reflexive/impersonal clitic placed before the verb; it marks a passive/reflexive construction. imbianchi is present subjunctive (third person singular) of imbiancare used reflexively (imbiancarsi) meaning “to whiten/whitewash,” here used figuratively “to whitewash/soften.”
segments.paradiso-8-g113: This segment is a single punctuation mark (a period) marking the end of the previous direct speech; there are no lexical items to analyze.
segments.paradiso-8-g114: E is the coordinating conjunction “and.” io is the first-person pronoun “I” and here introduces the speaker’s reply (followed by a colon in the text marking direct speech).
segments.paradiso-8-g115: Non is the simple negation “not.” già here is emphatic and with negation forms the idiom non già = “not at all” (già often means “indeed/already” but functions here as an emphatic particle).
segments.paradiso-8-g116: ché is a causal conjunction meaning “for/because” (also used to introduce an explanatory clause). impossibil is an adjective meaning “impossible” (predicate in the clause). veggio is the first-person present of vedere used in the sense “I see/find/consider.” che introduces the subordinate clause that follows. la is the definite article “the.” natura is a noun meaning “Nature” (here personified).
segments.paradiso-8-g117: in quel ch’è uopo is a prepositional phrase meaning “in that which is needed/in what is required.” in is the preposition “in.” quel is the demonstrative “that.” ch’ is the contracted form of che (relative pronoun “which/that”). è is the present indicative of essere (“is”). uopo is a noun meaning “need/requirement.” The whole phrase functions as complement of the verb phrase expressing what Nature cannot fail in.
segments.paradiso-8-g118: stanchi is a predicative adjective (masculine plural form) meaning “tired/defective.” In medieval/poetic usage collective or abstract nouns like natura can take plural adjective agreement; here stanchi is the predicate of the subordinate clause introduced by che and expresses the idea ‘be tired/be lacking.’
segments.paradiso-8-g119: Ond’ is a contracted form of onde/ond, used here as a discourse connector meaning “whereupon/therefore/then.” elli is an older/poetic form functioning as a third-person pronoun “he/it” (here the speaker), and ancora is the adverb “again/still,” indicating repetition.
segments.paradiso-8-g120: Or is an adverb meaning “now/then.” dì here is not the noun “day” but an archaic imperative form of dire (say) — so the phrase Or dì = “Now say.” (The provided lexical senses list for dì did not include the verbal imperative, hence that sense is marked as not listed.)
segments.paradiso-8-g121: sarebbe is the conditional (third person singular) of essere meaning “would be,” used to pose a hypothetical consequence. il peggio is a nominal phrase meaning “the worst” (peggio here as noun). per introduces the beneficiary/affected group “for/with respect to.” l’ omo in terra = “man on earth,” with the article l’ + omo (man) and in terra as a locative phrase “on earth/in the world.”
segments.paradiso-8-g122: se introduces the conditional clause “if.” non is the negation “not.” fosse is the imperfect subjunctive (third person singular) of essere, used in the protasis of a conditional to express a counterfactual/hypothetical: “if he were.” cive (from civo/civo) means “citizen” or “member of a city/community.”
segments.paradiso-8-g25: Indi = 'then' (temporal adverb). si fece is the reflexive use of fare in the passato remoto (si fece = 'he/she/it made himself' → 'came/placed himself'); l’un (l’ + un) means 'the one' or 'one of them'. più presso a noi = 'closer to us' (più = 'more', presso = 'near', a noi = 'to us'). e solo incominciò: 'and alone began' (solo as adverb 'alone/by himself' before the passato remoto incominciò).
segments.paradiso-8-g26: «Tutti sem presti = 'All of us are ready' (Tutti = all; sem is a contracted/poetic form of siamo = 'we are' in the present; presti = 'ready'/'prepared' used predicatively). al tuo piacer = 'to/for your pleasure' or idiomatically 'to do your will' (a + il = al; tuo piacer = your pleasure/pleasure of you).
segments.paradiso-8-g27: perché = 'because' (introduces the reason). di noi = 'in/from us' (di used with the pronoun to indicate relation). ti = clitic 'you' (direct/indirect object). gioi is an archaic form that does not match the provided sense list here (contextually it means something like 'you take joy/you rejoice in'); because the senses given do not include this meaning, it is marked -1.
segments.paradiso-8-g28: Noi ci volgiam = 'We turned ourselves' (Noi = we; ci = reflexive 'ourselves'; volgiam is an archaic/present form of volgere/volgere meaning 'we turn' or 'we were turning'). coi principi celesti = 'with the heavenly princes' (coi = con + i; principi celesti = heavenly 'princes' or 'principal beings'—note the provided sense list for 'principi' lacks 'princes', so that lexical item must be treated accordingly below). d’un giro e d’un girare e d’una sete = 'of one circuit and of one motion and of one thirst' — three coordinated nouns linked by the same preposition di (d’), expressing a single, shared rotation/movement and desire.
segments.paradiso-8-g29: ai quali = 'to whom' (preposition a + article + relative quale in plural); tu del mondo già dicesti = 'you already said, in the world' (del mondo = 'of the world' idiomatically 'when you were in the world'; già = already; dicesti = passato remoto, 2nd sing. 'you said').
segments.paradiso-8-g30: ‘Voi che ’ntendendo il terzo ciel movete’ = quoted line addressed to them: Voi = 'you' (plural). che = 'who/that'. ’ntendendo is the gerund of intendere (here 'understanding'/'perceiving' — the initial vowel is elided). il terzo ciel = 'the third heaven' (terzo = third; ciel = heaven/sky). movete = present indicative, 2nd plural 'you move' or 'you set in motion' — typical imagery of the celestial movers.
segments.paradiso-8-g31: e sem sì pien d’amor, = 'and we are so full of love' (sem = contracted siamo 'we are'; sì = 'so'; pien = 'full'; d’amor = 'of love').
segments.paradiso-8-g32: che = 'that' (introduces the result/consequence clause explaining the consequence of being full of love).
segments.paradiso-8-g33: per piacerti = 'to/for pleasing you' or 'in order to please you' (per = for/in order to; piacerti is the infinitive piacere with the enclitic ti = 'to please you').
segments.paradiso-8-g34: non fia men dolce un poco di quïete = 'a little repose will not be less sweet' (non = not; fia = future/archaic form of essere 'will be'; men dolce = 'less sweet'; un poco di quïete = 'a little quiet/repose'). The construction states that, even for the sake of pleasing you, a brief quiet will not be diminished in sweetness.
segments.paradiso-8-g35: Poscia che = 'after'/'afterwards that' (temporal subordinating phrase). li occhi miei si fuoro offerti = reflexive-passive periphrasis: li occhi miei 'my eyes'; si fuoro (archaic passato remoto plural of essere + si) + offerti (past participle) = 'were offered/upturned' or 'were presented' (the eyes were turned respectfully). a la mia donna reverenti = 'to my lady, reverent(ly)' (reverenti qualifies how the eyes were offered).
segments.paradiso-8-g36: ed essa fatti li avea di sé contenti e certi = 'and she had made them, by herself, content and certain' (ed = and; essa = she; fatti = past participle of fare used causatively 'made'; li = them; avea = imperfect 'had'; di sé = 'of herself'/'from herself' indicating the agent/source; contenti e certi = 'satisfied/content and sure/certain').
segments.paradiso-8-g37: rivolsersi a la luce che promessa tanto s’avea = 'they/they (turned) again toward the light which had been promised so much' (rivolsersi = reflexive 'to turn oneself back' or 'to turn toward'; a la luce = to the light; che promessa tanto s’avea = relative clause 'which so much had been promised' with s’avea = si aveva, impersonal/passive had been).
segments.paradiso-8-g38: e «Deh, = 'and "Ah,"' (e = and; Deh is an archaic interjection expressing appeal or exclamation, here 'Ah' or 'Oh').
segments.paradiso-8-g39: chi siete? = direct question 'who are you?' (chi = who; siete = 2nd plural present of essere 'are').
segments.paradiso-8-g40: fue la voce mia di grande affetto impressa = 'my voice was impressed with great affection/feeling' (fue = passato remoto of essere 'was'—archaic spelling; la voce mia = 'my voice'; di grande affetto = 'of great affection' describing the quality; impressa = past participle 'imprinted' agreeing with voce, forming a passive sense 'was impressed').
segments.paradiso-8-g131: Sì here means 'thus' or 'so' (drawing a conclusion from the previous argument). venne + gerund deducendo is a literary periphrasis: venne (past of venire) plus the gerund expresses that he 'came on' or 'proceeded' by deducing — i.e. he reasoned step by step. infino a quici is an adverbial phrase meaning 'as far as here' (quici = 'qui' with an enclitic element; it is an archaic/poetic form meaning 'here').
segments.paradiso-8-g132: poscia is an adverb meaning 'afterwards' or 'then'. conchiuse is the past (preterite) of conchiudere/concludere, 'he concluded' (here introducing reported speech).
segments.paradiso-8-g133: Dunque = 'therefore/so'. esser is the infinitive of essere used here as a predicate infinitive ('to be'), and diverse is an adjectival predicate 'different'. convien is an impersonal present (convien = 'it is necessary/appropriate') governing the infinitive phrase; di vostri effetti le radici is the delayed subject: 'the roots of your effects' (di introduces the genitive 'of'). Word order is inverted for poetic emphasis: literally 'Therefore it is necessary that the roots of your effects be different.'
segments.paradiso-8-g134: per ch’ is a fused form meaning 'wherefore/for which reason' (per + che). un here is an indefinite pronoun 'one' (meaning 'one person/case'); nasce is present indicative 'is born' or 'arises' used to state that such a type appears; Solone is the proper name Solon. The structure 'per ch’un nasce Solone e altro Serse' lists examples: 'one (a Solon) is born and another (a) Xerxes.'
segments.paradiso-8-g135: altro Melchisedèch e altro quello che continues the parallel listing: 'another (is) Melchizedek and another that one who...' altro = 'another', Melchisedèch a proper name. quello che is a demonstrative ('that one who') introducing a relative clause describing the last example.
segments.paradiso-8-g136: volando is the gerund of volare ('flying'), starting the relative clause that modifies quello 'that one who, flying...'. per l’aere = 'through/throughout the air' (per = 'through' here) and l’ aere = the air/sky; the gerund gives a circumstantial meaning 'while flying'.
segments.paradiso-8-g137: il figlio = 'the son' (definite); perse is the historical past of perdere ('lost'); in the clause the verb is perfective: 'lost his son' or 'the son was lost to him'. The subject of perse is the implied 'that one' (quello) from the previous segment.
segments.paradiso-8-g138: La circular natura = 'the circular (or revolving) nature'; circular is an adjective placed between article and noun in an older/poetic spelling. The phrase names nature conceived as cyclic or revolving.
segments.paradiso-8-g139: ch’è = che + è, 'which is'. suggello is a substantive 'a seal' or 'impression'. a la cera mortal literally 'on (to) mortal wax' — cera (wax) is a poetic metaphor for human nature; mortal is an adjective. Grammatically, the relative clause describes how circular nature stamps mortal wax.
segments.paradiso-8-g140: fa ben sua arte = 'does well its art': fa (does/makes) + ben (well) + sua arte (its skill/art). This is a straightforward verbal clause saying that nature performs its proper work effectively.
segments.paradiso-8-g141: ma non distingue l’un da l’altro ostello = 'but does not distinguish one lodging from another.' ma 'but' introduces the contrast; distingue is present indicative 'distinguishes'; l’un da l’altro ostello is an idiomatic expression 'one from the other inn/dwelling' — ostello here means a place or station (literally 'inn' but used generically).
segments.paradiso-8-g142: Quinci addivien = 'From this/hence it happens' (addivien = it happens, results). ch’Esaù si diparte = 'that Esau separates himself' — ch' = that; si diparte is reflexive dipartire 'to separate/depart (oneself)'. per seme da Iacòb means 'by seed/from the seed of Jacob' i.e. 'by lineage/descended from Jacob.'
segments.paradiso-8-g143: e vien Quirino = 'and Quirinus comes/arises' (vien = 'comes' or 'arises' in this figurative context). da sì vil padre = 'from so base a father' (da = 'from', sì = 'so', vil = 'base/lowly'). The phrase says Quirinus springs from a very ignoble father.
segments.paradiso-8-g144: che si rende a Marte = a relative clause introduced by che 'so that/which': si rende (riflessive) + rendere in this context means 'gives himself up/turns himself' (i.e. is devoted or delivered) a Marte 'to Mars' — figuratively 'to war' or bellicose tendencies. The verb is present indicative describing the result of having such a father.
segments.paradiso-8-g145: Natura generata = 'nature once generated' (generata is a past participle used adjectivally). il suo cammino = 'its course/path'. simil farebbe sempre = 'would do/make likewise always' — farebbe is the conditional 'would do', simil 'similarly' (poetic order), sempre 'always'. a’ generanti = 'toward the generators' or 'like the begetters' (a’ = archaic contraction of a + i, 'to the'). Grammatically, the clause states a general conditional tendency of generated nature.
segments.paradiso-8-g146: se non vincesse il proveder divino = 'if divine Providence did not prevail/overcome.' se = 'if' (conditional subordinating particle); non = 'not'; vincesse is the imperfect subjunctive of vincere ('would prevail/overcome' in this counterfactual clause); il proveder divino = 'divine Providence' (proveder from provvedere, used substantively). The clause gives the exception that prevents nature from always following its generators.
segments.paradiso-8-g147: Or = “now.” Quel che = “that which,” introducing a relative clause. t’era dietro = “was behind you”: the clitic t’ (ti) is attached to the imperfect era to mark the person affected (‘you’), and dietro means “behind.” t’è davanti = “is before you,” with the same clitic t’ attached to the present è; davanti = “in front/ahead.” The whole line states a change of position using simple past(imperfect)/present verbs with enclitic pronouns.
segments.paradiso-8-g148: Ma = “but.” Perché here introduces purpose (“so that”) rather than a question. Sappi is the present subjunctive (or imperative-form) of sapere used in a purpose clause: “that you may know.” Che begins the subordinate content clause. Di te = “from/of you” (di = “from” here), and mi giova = “it benefits me” (mi = clitic for ‘me’, giova = present indicative of giovare = “to benefit”), stating the reason or effect.
segments.paradiso-8-g149: Un corollario = “a corollary” (direct object fronted for emphasis). Voglio = present indicative first person singular “I want / I wish.” Che introduces a subordinate clause governed by this volition, so the verb there is subjunctive. t’ammanti is the clitic t’ + ammanti, the present subjunctive (3rd person sing.) meaning “may cloak/adorn you” (clitic attached to the verb is normal in older/poetic Italian); the verb form is archaic/poetic here and expresses Dante’s wish to clothe/adorn the addressee metaphorically.
segments.paradiso-8-g150: Sempre is an adverb meaning "always" placed before the noun for poetic emphasis; natura is the noun "nature" (subject) following the adverb. The normal prose order might be "la natura sempre", but the inversion is common in verse.
segments.paradiso-8-g151: Se introduces a conditional clause "if". Trova is present indicative of trovare ("finds"), here used in a general/habitual sense. Discorde is an adjective meaning "discordant/at odds" and a sé is a reflexive phrase "to itself/with itself"; syntactically this is "if Fortune finds (it) discordant to itself."
segments.paradiso-8-g152: Com' is a contracted form of come ("as/like"). Ogne is the archaic form of ogni ("every"), altra modifies semente ("seed") in the sense of offspring or thing sown. Fuor di means "outside of" (poetic variant of fuori di) and sua agrees with semente; regïon (with diaeresis) is "region/territory." Together: "like every other seed outside its own region."
segments.paradiso-8-g153: Fa is the present indicative of fare ("does/makes") and heads this short clause; mala prova is a fixed collocation meaning "a bad trial/result" or idiomatically "fares ill/turns out badly." The verb + noun here expresses the outcome: "makes a bad showing" or "has bad results."
segments.paradiso-8-g154: E means "and." Se begins a conditional clause; here ponesse is the imperfect subjunctive of porre ("to put/place"), used in the protasis of a contrary-to-fact or hypothetical conditional. 'Il mondo là giù' (the world down there) is the subject phrase, and 'ponesse mente a' is a fixed expression meaning "gave attention to" or "took to heart," so the clause means "if the world down there were to pay attention to the foundation that Nature lays."
segments.paradiso-8-g155: Seguendo is the gerund of seguire ("following"); it indicates manner or accompaniment and links to the main clause that follows. Lui is the tonic (stressed) pronoun used here as the object of the gerund (poetic/emphatic choice rather than a clitic), meaning "it/him" (referring back to the foundation or Nature).
segments.paradiso-8-g156: Avria is the present conditional of avere ("would have") used here in a semi-impersonal sense with a following predicative phrase; buona is a predicative adjective modifying la gente ("the people"). The idiomatic sense is "the people would be well/would fare well." The word order (verb + adjective + subject) is emphatic and common in poetry.
segments.paradiso-8-g157: Ma is a simple adversative conjunction ('but') introducing contrast. Voi torcete is 2nd-person-plural present indicative of torcere ('you twist/turn'), so the subject 'you' acts on 'religïone'. A la religïone = 'to/toward the religion' (la + noun); religïone shows a diaeresis on ï to indicate syllabic division for poetic meter. Tal che introduces a relative/result clause 'such that'; fia is the subjunctive present form of essere used in subordinate clauses (here meaning 'is/comes to be'), and nato is a past participle used adjectivally ('born, brought into being'). A + infinitive cignersi ('to gird oneself') uses the reflexive infinitive form (si attached) and takes la spada as its object ('the sword').
segments.paradiso-8-g158: E is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. Fate is 2nd-person-plural present of fare here meaning 'you make/appoint'. Re is the direct complement 'king'. Di tal = 'of such (a one)'; ch’ is the contracted form of che introducing a relative clause ('who/which'). È is the present indicative of essere ('is'), and da + noun (da sermone) is an idiomatic construction meaning 'fit for / belonging to [the pulpit/sermon]'; sermone therefore refers to preaching/sermoning rather than a literal object.
segments.paradiso-8-g159: Onde here functions as a conjunctive adverb meaning 'whence' or 'therefore' (introducing the result). La traccia vostra = 'your track/trace/path' (la + noun + possessive adjective). È is the present indicative 'is'. Fuor is an archaic or poetic form of fuori ('outside'/'off'), and fuor di strada = 'off the road' i.e. 'off course' or 'out of the right path'. The sentence ends with a closing quotation mark in the original.
segments.paradiso-8-g1: Solea + infinitive (creder) expresses a past habitual: 'used to believe.' Lo mondo = the world (lo is the article), in suo periclo = 'in its peril' where suo is a possessive referring back to mondo. Che introduces the subordinate clause reporting the content of that belief; raggiasse is an imperfect subjunctive (or poetically-shaped imperfect) used in reported speech after a past-tense main verb, meaning 'that ... shone / radiated.'
segments.paradiso-8-g2: Volta here is a past participle (feminine) modifying Ciprigna: 'turned' (i.e. was turned) — the phrase is elliptical, linking back to 'la bella Ciprigna.' Nel terzo epiciclo = 'into / in the third epicycle' (nel = in + the).
segments.paradiso-8-g3: Per che = 'and therefore / wherefore.' Non pur ... (ma ...) is the idiom 'not only ... but ...'; a lei faceano onore means 'paid honor to her' (faceano = imperfect they made/did). Ne l’antico errore 'in that ancient error' places the worship in the speaker’s judgment as mistaken.
segments.paradiso-8-g4: Ma introduces a contrast: 'but.' Onoravano is imperfect indicative, 'they honored / venerated' (ongoing past action); e connects Cupido as another object of their veneration.
segments.paradiso-8-g5: Quella = 'that one' (feminine, referring to Dïone) with per madre sua = 'as her mother' (per meaning 'as' or 'in the role of'); sua is a possessive agreeing with quella.
segments.paradiso-8-g6: Questo = 'this one' (masculine, i.e. Cupido) with per figlio = 'as (her) son.' The short clause contrasts the two deities by relation.
segments.paradiso-8-g7: E dicean = 'and they said' (imperfect habitual report). Ch’el sedette in grembo a Dido reports the mythic claim: ch’el = che + el ('that he'); sedette would be expected as the past form of sedere 'sat', but the provided sense list does not include that sense (see senses map). In grembo a Dido = 'in Dido’s lap/bosom.'
segments.paradiso-8-g8: E da costei = 'and from her' (costei = that woman/this one — here ‘her’), ond’ io principio piglio = 'from whom I take my beginning' (ond' = 'whence'), pigliavano il vocabol de la stella = 'they took the name/term of the star.' Che ’l sol vagheggia or da coppa or da ciglio = 'that the sun gazes/longs for, now from the cup, now from the brow' (or = 'now'). Note pigliavano is imperfect 'they took/took up.'
segments.paradiso-8-g9: Io non m’accorsi = 'I did not notice / I did not become aware': m’ is the reflexive clitic (mi) attached to accorsi (past form of accorgersi). Del salire = 'of the ascent' or 'that I had risen' (the article + infinitive is a common way to nominalize the action). In ella = 'into / in it' (ella = feminine demonstrative pronoun referring back to the place/thing).
segments.paradiso-8-g10: Ma = 'but.' D’esservi = contraction of di + esser + vi: 'of being there / of being in it' (esservi = to be in it). Entro reinforces 'within.' Mi fé assai fede = 'made me much faith' i.e. 'gave me abundant assurance' (fé = past tense 'made/caused', la donna mia is the agent). Ch’i’ vidi far più bella = 'that I saw become more beautiful' or 'whom I saw made more beautiful' (ch’ introduces a relative clause; vidi + infinitive far is a construction meaning 'I saw [her] become').
segments.paradiso-8-g11: E come = 'and as / just as.' In fiamma favilla si vede = impersonal/reflective construction 'a spark is seen in a flame' (si vede = 'is seen'); favilla is a small spark or ember visible within a flame.
segments.paradiso-8-g12: E come in voce voce si discerne = 'and as in one voice another voice is distinguished' — the repetition in voce voce emphasizes one voice perceived within another; si discerne is reflexive/passive 'is perceived / distinguished.'
segments.paradiso-8-g13: Quand’ una è ferma e altra va e riede = 'when one is fixed/still and another goes and returns.' È is present 'is'; ferma is adjectival 'still/fixed'; va e riede uses the present singular of andare and the archaic riedere 'to return', portraying alternating motion.
segments.paradiso-8-g14: Vid’ io = poetic contraction of vidi io 'I saw.' In essa luce = 'in that light' (essa = that/it). Altre lucerne muoversi in giro = the accusative infinitive construction vid' io ... muoversi = 'I saw other lamps move in a circle' (vidi + infinitive denotes perception of an action). Più e men correnti = 'more and less swift' (comparative manner: some ran faster, some slower).
segments.paradiso-8-g15: Al modo = 'in the manner' (al = a + il; modo = manner). It introduces a comparison with some internal rule.
segments.paradiso-8-g16: Credo = 'I believe' (first person present), a parenthetical comment signaling Dante’s modesty about the exactness of the simile.
segments.paradiso-8-g17: Di lor viste interne = 'of their inner visions' (di = of; lor = their; viste = visions/seeings; interne = internal). The genitive links 'the manner' to what is being compared: the inner seeing of those lights.
segments.paradiso-8-g18: Di fredda nube = 'from a cold cloud' (di + noun phrase indicating origin); non disceser venti (textually: 'there descended winds'): the syntax presents the source and the descending winds; venti is plural 'winds.'
segments.paradiso-8-g19: O visibili o no = 'whether visible or not' (o ... o ... = either/or); no here functions as 'not' (i.e. 'or not visible').
segments.paradiso-8-g20: Tanto festini = 'so great were their hastenings' — tanto qualifies festini (haste/hasteful motion). Festini is a poetic plural referring to rapid movements; the phrase emphasizes quickness.
segments.paradiso-8-g21: Che non paressero impediti e lenti a chi avesse quei lumi divini veduti a noi venir = 'that they would not appear hindered and slow to anyone who had seen those divine lights come toward us.' Che introduces the result/explanation; paressero is imperfect subjunctive/conditional in a past reporting context ('would seem'); a chi avesse ... veduti = 'to anyone who had seen' uses the pluperfect subjunctive/compound subjunctive construction to indicate a hypothetical observer in the past.
segments.paradiso-8-g22: Lasciando il giro pria cominciato in li alti Serafini = 'leaving the circle first begun among the high Seraphim.' Lasciando is a present participle 'leaving'; pria cominciato is a perfect participle phrase 'first begun before' modifying giro; in li alti Serafini names the place/agents where the circuit had been begun.
segments.paradiso-8-g23: E dentro a quei che più innanzi appariro sonava 'Osanna' sì = 'and within those who appeared more forward there sounded “Hosanna” so [loudly].' Dentro a quei = inside those; più innanzi = 'more in front / foremost'; sonava is imperfect 'there rang/sounded.' The quoted Osanna is the acclamation heard.
segments.paradiso-8-g24: Che unque poi di rïudir non fui sanza disiro = 'so that ever after I was not without desire to hear again.' Che introduces the resulting statement; unque poi = ever afterwards; rïudir (re-udire) = to hear again; non fui sanza disiro = 'I was not without desire' (fui = I was, sanza = without, disiro = desire).
segments.paradiso-8-g55: Quella sinistra riva is a demonstrative noun phrase ('that left bank'). The relative clause che si lava uses the reflexive/passive si with the present indicative lava to mean 'is washed' (passive sense). Di marks the agent/source ('by the Rhône'), poi ch' is an archaic/contracted temporal/causal connector ('after' / 'when') and è misto con uses essere + past participle (misto) to mean 'is mingled with' (ch' + è = ch'è).
segments.paradiso-8-g56: Per suo segnore is a prepositional phrase 'for/on behalf of its lord'; a tempo is a temporal adverbial 'at the appointed time'. M'aspettava is the imperfect of aspettare with the clitic m' (mi) before the verb, giving 'was awaiting / awaited me' — the imperfect gives a past, ongoing/past-habitual sense.
segments.paradiso-8-g57: E quel corno d’Ausonia is another demonstrative noun phrase ('and that horn/cape of Ausonia'); the relative clause che s'imborga uses the pronominal verb s'imborga (si + imborga) meaning 'is surrounded/enclosed (by towns)' or 'is ringed with' — s' indicates the reflexive/pronominal form. The repeated di + place names lists the towns enclosing it.
segments.paradiso-8-g58: Da ove is an archaic locution meaning 'from where' (ove = where). Tronto e Verde are the river names; in mare sgorga uses the present indicative sgorga ('gushes/flows out') with in mare ('into the sea'). Note the verb is singular (sgorga) despite two rivers, a poetic/licensed agreement choice.
segments.paradiso-8-g59: Fulgeami is fulgea + mi (an archaic clitic form) meaning 'shone on me' (verb + enclitic object). Già is 'already'; in fronte marks the place on the body ('on my brow/forehead'). La corona is the subject ('the crown'). The relative clause di quella terra che 'l Danubio riga uses riga (here a verb) to mean 'the Danube streaks/furrows/marks' the land, and poi che le ripe tedesche abbandona is a temporal clause 'after it leaves the German banks' (abbandona = present/past sense here with temporal force).
segments.paradiso-8-g60: E la bella Trinacria is a simple coordinated noun phrase ('And fair Trinacria') functioning as the sentence subject; the initial e simply links this clause to what precedes.
segments.paradiso-8-g61: Che caliga is a relative clause with caliga (present indicative of caligare) meaning 'which grows dark/misty/dim'. Tra Pachino e Peloro gives the location 'between Pachino and Pelorus' (Peloro = Cape Pelorus).
segments.paradiso-8-g62: Sopra 'l golfo locates Trinacria 'above the gulf'; che riceve is a relative clause 'which receives/experiences' with riceve (present indicative). Da Euro indicates source/agent 'from the Euro' (here Eurus, the east wind), and maggior briga = 'greater trouble' or 'greater disturbance' (maggior modifies briga).
segments.paradiso-8-g63: Non per Tifeo ma per nascente solfo gives the causal contrast: 'not because of Typhon but because of the rising sulphur.' Per marks cause; nascente is an adjective 'rising' and solfo is a volcanic/sulphurous substance. Tifeo here is the mythic Typhon (not the medical sense).
segments.paradiso-8-g64: Attesi avrebbe is an inverted order of auxiliary + past participle (avrebbe attesi), a poetic inversion that produces the conditional perfect 'would have awaited.' Attesi is the past participle of attendere placed before the auxiliary; li suoi regi ancora ('its kings still') are the persons referred to (the participle agrees in number with them).
segments.paradiso-8-g65: Nati per me is a past participle phrase 'born for me' that modifies/liens to li suoi regi; di Carlo e di Ridolfo indicates parentage or origin 'of Charles and of Rudolf.' Nati agrees in number with regi (plural).
segments.paradiso-8-g66: Se introduces a conditional ('if'); mala segnoria is a noun phrase 'evil/ill lordship' (segnoria = dominion, rule). This is the protasis of a counterfactual condition referring to what follows.
segments.paradiso-8-g67: Che sempre accora is a relative clause ('which always enrages / makes sore'); accora is a present-tense verb meaning to inflame, enrage, or distress. Li popoli suggetti is the object phrase 'the subject peoples' affected by that lordship.
segments.paradiso-8-g68: Non avesse mosso is the pluperfect/subjunctive construction ('had not moved' / 'had not stirred') used in the counterfactual clause; mosso is the past participle placed after the auxiliary (typical sequence). Palermo a gridar means 'Palermo to shout/call out' (a + infinitive expresses purpose/result here).
segments.paradiso-8-g69: “Mora is an exclamation quoted here; in this context it is a shouted cry. The exact historical sense is debated, but it functions as an imperative/exclamatory call.
segments.paradiso-8-g70: mora!” repeats the exclamation for emphasis; the doubled cry is a rhetorical/choral effect in the passage.
segments.paradiso-8-g71: This segment is punctuation marking the end of the passage (a period).
segments.paradiso-8-g72: E se introduces a hypothetical clause ('And if ...'). mio frate places the possessive before the noun in the usual Italian order ('my brother'). antivedesse is the third-person imperfect subjunctive (archaic/poetic form of antivedere) used in the protasis of a counterfactual/hypothetical construction, equivalent to 'had foreseen' or 'were to foresee'.
segments.paradiso-8-g73: l’ (elided article) + avara povertà is a noun phrase 'the avaricious/mean poverty' (an idiomatic description). di Catalogna gives origin/possession 'of Catalonia'. già is an adverb 'already'. fuggeria is an archaic/metered verb form expressing the conditional/result ('would have fled' or 'would already have fled'); the -eria ending is older/poetic.
segments.paradiso-8-g74: perché here functions as a purpose/result connector 'so that / in order that' (not a question). non negates the subordinate clause. li is the old clitic pronoun referring back to 'him' (mio frate); offendesse is the imperfect subjunctive of offendere used in a subordinate purpose/result clause ('would offend' / 'should offend').
segments.paradiso-8-g75: ché = 'for' / 'because' giving a reason; veramente 'truly'. proveder (infinitive) + bisogna (impersonal verb) form the periphrasis 'bisogna provveder' → 'it is necessary to provide'; the construction is impersonal (there is no grammatical subject). per lui = 'for him'.
segments.paradiso-8-g76: o = 'or' offering an alternative; per altrui = 'for others' / 'on behalf of others' (altrui is a genitive/pronominal form meaning 'other people's' / 'others'). The phrase balances the previous 'per lui'.
segments.paradiso-8-g77: sì ch’ is a result connector 'so that' / 'such that'. a sua barca = 'upon his boat' (a + poss. adjective + noun). carcata is a past-participial/adjectival use of carcare 'laden' / 'loaded'. più d’incarco = 'more than a charge/duty' (incarco = burden/responsibility). non si pogna is an archaic clause: non negates, si is the reflexive/impersonal particle, and pogna (archaic pognare) here means 'be placed' / 'be laid on' or 'be borne' — so the clause means 'may not be loaded/laid on'. overall result: 'so that his already-laden boat cannot be given more than its charge.'
segments.paradiso-8-g78: La sua natura is a noun phrase 'his nature/disposition' (la + possessive + noun); natura here means temperament or character rather than 'natural world'.
segments.paradiso-8-g79: che is the relative pronoun 'which'; di indicates origin 'from'; larga here means 'bountiful / generous' modifying parca; parca refers to a Fate (one of the Parcae—the Fates) rather than 'sparing', and discese is a past form 'descended' (poetic/historical past) agreeing with natura.
segments.paradiso-8-g80: avria is the archaic conditional ('would have'); mestier (from mestiere) means 'need' here ('would have need'); di tal milizia = 'of such a militia/force' (tal = such). che introduces a relative/result clause: non curasse (imperfect subjunctive used in a potential/hypothetical sense) 'would not care / would not bother' di mettere in arca 'to put into the chest/ark' — 'in arca' means into a chest or treasury. The whole says his nature would require such force that it would not concern itself with putting things into the chest.
segments.paradiso-8-g81: Però ch’ = 'for / because that' introducing a reason; i’ credo = 'I believe' (archaic i’ = io). l’alta letizia = 'the high/lofty joy' (article + adjective + noun). che introduces a relative clause modifying letizia; ’l tuo parlar = 'your speech' with elided article + possessive; m’infonde is 'pours into me / instills in me' (m’ = mi indirect object; infonde = present indicative).
segments.paradiso-8-g82: segnor mio is a vocative address 'my lord' (archaic spelling segnor = signor). The order is vocative + possessive for emphasis.
segments.paradiso-8-g83: là ’ve = 'there where' (là + ve, with ve = where). ogne ben = 'every good' (ogne = ogni). si termina e s’inizia are reflexive/impersonal verbs: 'is completed/ends and is begun' (the si marks the reflexive/impersonal voice), describing the place 'where every good both ends and begins.'
segments.paradiso-8-g84: per te = 'through you' / 'by means of you'. si veggia is an impersonal/passive-like form ('is seen' / 'is perceived') with veggia an archaic verbal form; come la vegg’ io = 'as I see it' where la refers back to 'letizia' (the thing seen) and vegg' (vegg = archaic 'see') is first-person present 'I see'. The clause means 'is seen through you as I see it.'
segments.paradiso-8-g85: grata m’è più = 'it is more pleasing to me' (grata = pleasing; m’ = to me; è = is; più = more). The adjective agrees with the feminine idea 'la letizia'.
segments.paradiso-8-g86: e anco = 'and also'; quest’ ho caro = an idiom 'I hold this dear / I value this' (ho + caro). perché = 'because'. ’l (lo) discerni = 'you discern it' (discerni is 2nd singular present 'you discern' addressed to the addressee). rimirando (gerund) 'by gazing/ contemplating' in Dio = 'in God' (meaning when you contemplate in God or in the presence of God).
segments.paradiso-8-g87: Fatto m’hai lieto is a perfect construction: fatto (past participle) + hai (auxiliary) + adjective lieto = 'you have made me glad.' The past participle is used predicatively with the auxiliary to form the result on 'me' (m’ = to me).
segments.paradiso-8-g88: e così mi fa chiaro = 'and thus it makes clear to me' (fa chiaro = 'renders clear', with mi as indirect object 'to me'). così is 'so/thus' and chiaro is adjective 'clear'.
segments.paradiso-8-g89: poi che is a conjunction meaning 'since / in that' giving the reason for the previous statement ('since...').
segments.paradiso-8-g90: parlando is a gerund 'speaking' indicating the circumstance in which the following effect occurred ('in speaking').
segments.paradiso-8-g91: a dubitar = 'to doubting' (a + infinitive used to express the effect/purpose); m’hai mosso = 'you have moved/caused me' (present perfect). com’ esser può = 'how it can be' (com’ = come = how; esser = essere 'to be'; può = can/may). The clause: 'you have caused me to doubt how this can be.'
segments.paradiso-8-g92: di dolce seme = 'from a sweet seed / of sweet origin' (di = from/of; dolce = sweet; seme = seed/origin). The phrase expresses the surprising origin from which the bitterness comes.
segments.paradiso-8-g93: amaro is the predicate adjective 'bitter' describing the unexpected result — succinct closing: 'bitter.'

sentences.paradiso-8-s9: And how great and of what kind I saw her grow more from a new joy that increased within her, when I spoke, toward her own rejoicings!
sentences.paradiso-8-s10: Thus made, she said to me: "The world held me below for only a short time; and if I had been longer, there would be much harm that would not be."
sentences.paradiso-8-s11: My joy keeps you hidden from me; it shines around me and hides me, as if wrapped like an animal in its own silk.
sentences.paradiso-8-s12: You loved me greatly, and you had good reason; for if I had been down there, I would have shown you more of my love beyond the leaves.
sentences.paradiso-8-s21: I said this to him; and he to me: "If I can show you a truth, you will keep your face as you keep your back."
sentences.paradiso-8-s22: The good that all the realm which you traverse turns and satisfies makes its providence be a virtue in these great bodies.
sentences.paradiso-8-s23: And not only are providential natures in the mind which is perfect of itself, but they are together with their salvation: therefore although this arrow prepared falls to a provident end, just as a thing directed to its mark.
sentences.paradiso-8-s24: If that were not so, the heaven in which you walk would so produce its effects that they would be not arts but ruins; and that cannot be, if the intellects that move these stars are not lacking, and much less the first one, which does not have them perfect.
sentences.paradiso-8-s30: 'Yes,' I answered; 'and here I ask no argument.'
sentences.paradiso-8-s31: 'And could it be, if down below one does not live differently by means of different offices?'
sentences.paradiso-8-s32: No, if your master writes it well.
sentences.paradiso-8-s25: Do you want me to whitewash this truth for you any further?
sentences.paradiso-8-s27: And I: "Not at all; for I see it impossible that Nature, in what is required, should be tired."
sentences.paradiso-8-s28: Whereupon he again: "Now say: would it be the worst for man on earth if he were not a citizen?"
sentences.paradiso-8-s5: Then one of them drew nearer to us and, by himself, began:
sentences.paradiso-8-s6: We turned ourselves with the heavenly princes in one circle, with one motion and one thirst; to whom you already said in the world: 'You who, understanding, move the third heaven'; and we are so full of love that, to please you, a little repose will not be less sweet.
sentences.paradiso-8-s7: After my eyes were reverently offered to my lady, and she had made them satisfied and assured by herself, they (or she) turned back to the light that had been promised so much, and, 'Ah, who are you?'
sentences.paradiso-8-s8: My voice was stamped with (or filled with) great feeling.
sentences.paradiso-8-s33: So he proceeded deducing up to here; then he concluded: "Therefore the roots of your effects must be different: wherefore one like Solon is born and another Xerxes, another Melchizedek and another that one who, flying through the air, lost his son."
sentences.paradiso-8-s34: Circular nature, which is a seal upon mortal wax, does its art well, but it does not distinguish one dwelling from another.
sentences.paradiso-8-s35: Hence it comes about that Esau separates himself by seed from Jacob; and Quirinus comes from so base a father that he gives himself over to Mars.
sentences.paradiso-8-s36: Generated nature would always follow the same course as the begetters, if divine Providence did not prevail.
sentences.paradiso-8-s37: Now that which was behind you is before you; but so that you may know that I profit from you, I would like to cloak/adorn you with a corollary.
sentences.paradiso-8-s38: Nature, whenever Fortune is at odds with it, like any other seed outside its proper region, fares poorly.
sentences.paradiso-8-s39: And if the world down there paid heed to the foundation that Nature lays, and followed it, people would fare well.
sentences.paradiso-8-s40: But you twist religion so that it is born to gird the sword, and you make king of one who belongs to the pulpit; therefore your track is off the road.
sentences.paradiso-8-s1: The world used to believe that, in its peril, beautiful Cyprian (Venus) shed the mad love, turned into the third epicycle; and therefore not only did the ancient peoples pay her the honor of sacrifice and votive cries in that old error, but they honored Dione and Cupid — the one as her mother, the other as her son — and they said that he sat in Dido’s lap; and from her, from whom I take my origin, they took the name of the star that the sun yearns for, now from the cup, now from the brow.
sentences.paradiso-8-s2: I did not notice my rising into it; but my lady gave me abundant proof that I was within it, — my lady whom I saw become even more beautiful.
sentences.paradiso-8-s3: And as a spark is seen in a flame, and as one voice is distinguished within another — when one is still and another goes and returns — I saw in that light other lamps moving around, swifter and slower, in the manner, I believe, of their inward visions.
sentences.paradiso-8-s4: From a cold cloud there descended winds — visible or not — so hurried in their haste that they would not seem impeded or slow to anyone who had seen those divine lights coming toward us, leaving the circuit first begun among the high Seraphim; and within those who appeared foremost there sounded 'Hosanna' so that ever after I was not without desire to hear again.
sentences.paradiso-8-s13: That left bank, which is washed by the Rhône after it is mingled with the Sorga, awaited me at the appointed time for its lord, and that horn of Ausonia which is enclosed by Bari and by Gaeta and by Catona, from where the Tronto and the Verde gush into the sea.
sentences.paradiso-8-s14: Already the crown of that land, which the Danube streaks, shone upon my brow after it abandons the German banks.
sentences.paradiso-8-s15: And fair Trinacria, which grows dim between Pachino and Pelorus, above the gulf that receives greater trouble from the Euro, not from Typhon but from the rising sulphur, would still have awaited its kings born for me of Charles and of Rudolf, if ill lordship, which always enrages the subject peoples, had not stirred Palermo to cry: 'Mora, mora!'
sentences.paradiso-8-s17: And if my brother had foreseen this, the stingy poverty of Catalonia would already have fled, so as not to offend him; for truly one must provide for him, or for others, so that his boat, already laden, may not be burdened with more than its charge.
sentences.paradiso-8-s18: His disposition, which descended from a generous Parca, would have need of such a force that it would not care to put things into the chest.
sentences.paradiso-8-s19: For I believe that the lofty joy which your speech pours into me, my lord — there where every good both ends and begins — is seen through you as I see it, and is more pleasing to me; and I also hold this dear because you discern it by gazing upon it in God.
sentences.paradiso-8-s20: You have made me glad, and so you make it clear to me; for, speaking, you have set me to doubt how it can be that from a sweet seed there should come forth bitterness.
